symptomSummary: After a prompt revision accumulates ~30+ invocations, evolve ignores recent negative feedback and generates regressions instead of fixes.
rootCauseHypothesis: The invocation query for a revision sorts ascending by date and then applies a hard limit of 25, so once more than 25 invocations exist the query returns only the oldest 25 — every recent thumbs-down signal falls outside the window and is silently discarded. The ~30-invocation threshold maps directly to exceeding this 25-row cap.
fixHypothesis: Flip the sort order in `InvocationRepository.forRevision` from ascending to descending so the query returns the most-recent N invocations instead of the oldest N; optionally also surface the `telemetryWindow` default (currently 25 in `src/index.ts`) as a tunable if larger windows are desirable.

primaryLocation.rationale: The query `.orderBy(asc(schema.invocations.date)).limit(limit)` fetches the oldest `limit` (default 25) invocations for a revision. Once invocation count exceeds 25, all newer invocations — and their associated signals — are never returned to the evolve pipeline. Changing `asc` to `desc` makes the window track recency correctly.

candidateLocations.rationale: The `evolve` function passes `telemetryWindow` (default 25) to `store.invocationsForRevision`. Raising this default is a complementary fix, but the root ordering bug in the repository means a larger limit would still return old data first.
`forArtifact` correctly uses `desc` ordering — confirming the `asc` in `forRevision` is an inconsistency rather than deliberate design.
If the Store interface or KV-backed implementation also defines `invocationsForRevision`, it may share the same ascending-sort defect and need a parallel fix for non-SQLite stores.

KV store implementations often accumulate entries in insertion order with no guaranteed sort; if evolve can run against the KV backend, its invocation retrieval may lack a recency sort and exhibit the same symptom.
